ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general shrimp food product class; no specific entry for Cantonese white-poached shrimp preparation exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers shrimps and prawns as a category; no preparation-specific code for white-poached shrimp. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 identifies shrimp as a substance; no specific code for the Cantonese poached preparation. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of white-poached shrimp consists of approximately 6 to 8 large whole prawns, weighing around 200 g including shells, served with a soy-sesame dipping sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, cooked, moist heat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 15152)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Bai Zhuo Da Xia is prepared by blanching/poaching large fresh shrimp in lightly seasoned water. USDA SR Legacy values for moist-heat cooked shrimp (NDB 15152 / FDC 175180) are used as the primary nutrient basis for the shrimp component per 100 g edible portion. Sodium is adjusted upward modestly to reflect typical soy-sesame dipping sauce contribution absorbed during eating; the shrimp itself is low-sodium. Values represent the edible shrimp flesh after poaching, excluding shell weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bai Zhuo Da Xia. Shrimp and other shellfish are high-protein, very low-carbohydrate foods. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) classify seafood and shellfish as having a GI of approximately 5, consistent with other very low-carbohydrate protein foods. GI of 5 used as analogous estimate; gi_category is low (≤55). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (5 × 0.9 × 2) / 100 = 0.09, rounded to 0.1.

hypertension.guidance: The shrimp flesh itself is relatively low in sodium (~224 mg/100 g cooked), but the traditional soy-sesame dipping sauce served alongside can significantly increase total sodium intake; individuals with hypertension should use the dipping sauce sparingly or request a reduced-sodium version.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bai Zhuo Da Xia is an excellent protein source with negligible carbohydrate (~0.9 g/100 g) and a very low glycemic index (~5), making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes seeking to manage postprandial blood glucose levels.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol content offset by low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Shrimp contains relatively high dietary cholesterol (~189 mg/100 g) but is low in saturated fat and provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids; current evidence suggests moderate shrimp consumption is acceptable for most individuals with cardiovascular disease, though those with strict dietary cholesterol restrictions should monitor portion size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bai Zhuo Da Xia (白灼大虾 / 白灼大蝦), literally 'white-blanched large shrimp', is a classic Cantonese dish originating from Guangdong province, China, and widely popular across Chinese-speaking communities in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Southeast Asia. The dish is prepared by briefly poaching or blanching whole large fresh shrimp or prawns in lightly seasoned boiling water — sometimes with ginger slices and spring onion — until just cooked through, preserving the natural sweetness, firm texture, and clean flavour of the shrimp. The shrimp are typically served whole in their shells, accompanied by a dipping sauce of light soy sauce, sesame oil, and sometimes minced ginger or chilli. The cooking method is deliberately minimal to highlight ingredient quality. Nutritionally, the dish is high in lean protein (~21 g/100 g edible flesh), very low in carbohydrates (~0.9 g/100 g), and low in fat (~1.7 g/100 g), making it one of the most nutritionally efficient seafood dishes in Chinese cuisine. It provides meaningful amounts of selenium, phosphorus, vitamin B12, and potassium. The glycemic index is very low (~5), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The primary clinical consideration is sodium from the dipping sauce, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at Cantonese seafood restaurants and zi char stalls as a shared dish.
common_user_queries: What is bai zhuo da xia?
Is blanched shrimp good for diabetics?
Sodium content of bai zhuo xia
Is bai zhuo da xia high in cholesterol?
meal_context: shared dish at Chinese seafood restaurant

confidence_reason: The dish is a well-documented Cantonese preparation. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy moist-heat cooked shrimp data (FDC 175180), which is a strong analogous match for the edible shrimp flesh component. No Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry for this exact dish was identified. GI is estimated from peer-reviewed literature for shellfish as a food category. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of an exact dish-level entry in a primary regional source.
notes: 1. food_group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Fish and seafood', which is the correct classification for a shrimp dish. 2. Nutrient values are per 100 g edible shrimp flesh (post-poaching, shell excluded), adapted from USDA FDC 175180 (shrimp, cooked, moist heat). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source for cooked shrimp. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as shrimp contains no dietary fibre. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked shrimp (FDC 175180). 5. Sodium value of 224 mg/100 g reflects the USDA value for moist-heat cooked shrimp; actual sodium intake will be higher if dipping sauce is consumed. 6. GI of 5 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) classification of shellfish/seafood as very low GI foods. 7. Cholesterol is high at 189 mg/100 g, which is clinically relevant for cardiovascular disease management. 8. The field dataed (Bei Jiang Restaurant 北疆饭店) is a Northern Chinese restaurant; however, Bai Zhuo Da Xia is a Cantonese preparation widely served across Chinese restaurant styles in Singapore. 9. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.
